summary: McKenzie visualizes his SVN commit history to understand his own work patterns, creating graphs of commits per month and lines of code over time. He discovers he works in spurts driven by inspiration, prefers shipping v1.0 quickly and iterating, and sometimes loses momentum when his day job demands energy. He also graphs cumulative lines of code, showing steady growth over years. The exercise reveals that code output alone doesn't capture the full picture of running a software business.
key_points: Visualizing commit history reveals personal work patterns: McKenzie works in inspiration-driven spurts rather than consistently
His preferred approach is shipping a minimal v1.0 and iterating rapidly rather than building something ambitious upfront
Code output is an incomplete measure of business progress — significant work happens outside of writing code
Simple tools (Ruby scripts, Excel, SVN logs) can provide useful self-knowledge about work habits
money_quotes: I tend to work in spurts when I have inspiration.
I prefer to ship v1.0 of something and iterate like mad rather than spending more time shipping something more ambitious to start with.
I sometimes get slammed by the day job and am left with no energy for the business.
Code is not everything in the software business.
Loading the entire Rails framework 1,334 times will not make your CPU happy.
I guess slow and steady wins the race.
key_insight: Visualizing your own work patterns reveals that running a software business involves far more than writing code, and that shipping quickly and iterating beats perfectionism.
